
Graphics Engine Bible 2026
Volume II — Rendering Systems & Physically Based Rendering

Chapter 3: Forward+ and Clustered Rendering

=====================================================================
3.1 Introduction
=====================================================================

Forward+ and Clustered Rendering represent the evolution of traditional
forward rendering. These techniques solve one of the largest problems
in real-time graphics:

    Efficiently rendering scenes containing hundreds or thousands
    of dynamic lights.

Traditional forward rendering scales poorly because every object must
evaluate every light affecting it.

Forward+ and Clustered Rendering solve this problem through intelligent
light culling and GPU-driven light management.

=====================================================================
3.2 Traditional Forward Rendering
=====================================================================

Classic forward rendering operates as follows:

For each object:
    For each light:
        Evaluate lighting

Complexity:

    O(Objects × Lights)

As light counts increase, performance rapidly degrades.

Example:

1000 Objects
500 Lights

Potential evaluations:

500,000 lighting operations

This becomes unsustainable for modern scenes.

=====================================================================
3.3 Evolution Toward Forward+
=====================================================================

Forward+ introduces screen-space light culling.

Instead of evaluating all lights:

1. Divide screen into tiles
2. Determine which lights affect each tile
3. Evaluate only relevant lights

Result:

Massive reduction in lighting calculations.

=====================================================================
3.4 Forward+ Architecture
=====================================================================

Pipeline:

Depth Prepass
      |
      V
Build Tile Light Lists
      |
      V
Forward Lighting Pass
      |
      V
Post Processing

The GPU first determines which lights influence each region of the
screen before shading begins.

=====================================================================
3.5 Tile-Based Rendering
=====================================================================

The screen is divided into small rectangular regions.

Common sizes:

8x8 pixels
16x16 pixels
32x32 pixels

Example:

1920 x 1080 resolution

Using 16x16 tiles:

120 × 68 tiles

Total:

8160 tiles

Each tile maintains its own light list.

=====================================================================
3.6 Tile Frustums
=====================================================================

Each tile corresponds to a frustum in view space.

These frustums are used to determine which lights intersect the tile.

Simplified workflow:

Create tile frustum
Test all lights
Store intersecting lights

The resulting list is significantly smaller than the total scene light
count.

=====================================================================
3.7 Compute Shader Light Culling
=====================================================================

Modern implementations use compute shaders.

Workflow:

Dispatch Compute Shader
Generate Tile Frustums
Cull Lights
Store Light Indices

Benefits:

- Massive parallelism
- Reduced CPU workload
- Better scalability

Example data structure:

struct TileLightList
{
    uint Count;
    uint LightIndices[256];
};

=====================================================================
3.8 Light Bounding Volumes
=====================================================================

Lights are represented by bounding volumes.

Point Light:
    Sphere

Spot Light:
    Cone

Area Light:
    Box or custom volume

Testing becomes:

Light intersects tile?

If true:

Add light index to tile list.

=====================================================================
3.9 Depth-Aware Forward+
=====================================================================

Basic Forward+ uses only screen-space dimensions.

Problem:

Large depth ranges create inefficient light lists.

Example:

Foreground object
Background mountain

Same tile
Very different depths

Solution:

Depth-aware partitioning.

=====================================================================
3.10 Motivation for Clustered Rendering
=====================================================================

Clustered Rendering extends Forward+ into 3D space.

Instead of:

Tile(X,Y)

We use:

Cluster(X,Y,Z)

This dramatically improves light assignment precision.

=====================================================================
3.11 Cluster Construction
=====================================================================

Clusters divide view space into:

Width
Height
Depth

Example:

120 x 68 x 24

Clusters:

195,840

Each cluster stores:

- Light references
- Reflection probes
- Decals
- Volumetric data

=====================================================================
3.12 Depth Slicing
=====================================================================

Depth slicing is critical.

Linear slicing:

Simple
Poor distribution

Logarithmic slicing:

Preferred
More precision near camera

Common formula:

Slice = log(depth)

Advantages:

Better utilization
Improved culling
Reduced overdraw

=====================================================================
3.13 Cluster Generation Pipeline
=====================================================================

Step 1:
Build cluster grid

Step 2:
Generate bounding volumes

Step 3:
Cull lights

Step 4:
Store cluster light lists

Step 5:
Render scene

All steps can be GPU-driven.

=====================================================================
3.14 GPU Data Structures
=====================================================================

Typical cluster data:

struct Cluster
{
    uint Offset;
    uint Count;
};

struct LightIndex
{
    uint Index;
};

Benefits:

Compact memory usage
Cache-friendly access
Fast traversal

=====================================================================
3.15 Memory Considerations
=====================================================================

Clustered rendering trades memory for performance.

Resources include:

Cluster Buffers
Light Lists
Index Buffers
Visibility Data

Optimization strategies:

Compression
Sparse allocation
Dynamic resizing

=====================================================================
3.16 Forward+ vs Deferred Rendering
=====================================================================

Forward+ Advantages:

- Better MSAA support
- Transparency compatibility
- Lower memory consumption
- Simpler material evaluation

Deferred Advantages:

- Flexible G-buffer workflows
- Screen-space effect integration
- Consistent material storage

Choice depends on engine requirements.

=====================================================================
3.17 Forward+ vs Clustered Rendering
=====================================================================

Forward+

Advantages:
- Simpler implementation
- Lower memory cost

Disadvantages:
- Less precise culling

Clustered

Advantages:
- Better scalability
- Improved depth precision
- Volumetric compatibility

Disadvantages:
- Increased complexity

=====================================================================
3.18 Transparency Rendering
=====================================================================

Forward+ excels at transparency.

Examples:

Glass
Particles
Smoke
Hair
Water

Because shading occurs directly during rasterization, transparency
integration is significantly easier than in deferred pipelines.

=====================================================================
3.19 Volumetric Rendering Integration
=====================================================================

Clustered systems naturally support volumetrics.

Data stored per cluster:

Fog density
Participating media
Scattering coefficients

Applications:

Volumetric fog
Cloud rendering
Atmospheric effects

=====================================================================
3.20 Reflection Probe Management
=====================================================================

Clusters can store reflection probe references.

Benefits:

Efficient lookup
Reduced probe searches
Improved cache locality

Common in large open-world engines.

=====================================================================
3.21 GPU-Driven Rendering
=====================================================================

Modern engines increasingly adopt GPU-driven workflows.

Examples:

Indirect Draws
Mesh Shaders
Task Shaders
GPU Visibility Systems

Clustered rendering integrates naturally with these architectures.

=====================================================================
3.22 Production Engine Usage
=====================================================================

Many modern engines employ variations of:

Forward+
Clustered Forward
Clustered Deferred
Hybrid Renderers

Reasons:

Scalability
Memory efficiency
Compatibility with modern hardware

=====================================================================
3.23 Optimization Strategies
=====================================================================

Recommendations:

Use logarithmic depth slicing.
Cull lights on GPU.
Compress cluster data.
Minimize memory bandwidth.
Reuse visibility results.
Profile cluster occupancy.

Avoid excessive cluster counts that waste memory and cache resources.

=====================================================================
3.24 Future Directions
=====================================================================

Forward+ and Clustered Rendering continue evolving alongside:

Mesh Shaders
Ray Tracing
Neural Rendering
GPU-Driven Pipelines

Future renderers will likely combine:

Clustered Lighting
Ray-Traced Effects
Temporal Reconstruction
Machine Learning Acceleration

=====================================================================
3.25 Summary
=====================================================================

Forward+ and Clustered Rendering represent some of the most important
advances in real-time rendering architecture.

By intelligently culling lights and organizing scene data, these
systems enable modern engines to render massive worlds with thousands
of dynamic light sources while maintaining real-time performance.

End of Chapter 3.
